ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for beef yakiniku donburi; FOODON_00002572 (beef dish) used as the closest defensible broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Beef based dishes) is the closest available category; no specific yakiniku donburi code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Japanese beef bowl preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising steamed white rice (~200 g), grilled beef slices (~120 g), two half-boiled eggs (~100 g), and yakiniku sauce (~30 g).

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Double Half Boiled Egg Beef Yakiniku Bowl (G00010200)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 55 kcal/100g, protein 10.7 g/100g, carbohydrate 2.36 g/100g) taken directly from HPB Foodo record G00010200. Fat reported as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source; this is flagged as anomalous for a beef-and-egg dish and is documented in notes. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, fibre, sodium, sugar) estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite of grilled beef (USDA #13365), hard-boiled egg (USDA #01129), and yakiniku sauce analogue, weighted to the HPB macro profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef yakiniku bowl. GI estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database) diluted by the high-protein beef and egg components (~60% of dish weight), which substantially lower the composite meal GI. Composite meal GI estimated using the weighted-carbohydrate method: (rice carbohydrate fraction × GI 72) adjusted for protein-mediated insulin response attenuation, yielding an estimated composite GI of ~54. GI 54 falls in the low category (≤55). GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving g) / 100 = (54 × 9.44) / 100 ≈ 5.1 g, where available carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 2.36 × 4 = 9.44 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from yakiniku sauce (soy sauce base); estimated ~340 mg sodium per 100 g, translating to approximately 1,360 mg per full 400 g bowl — approaching the daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request yakiniku sauce on the side or reduced quantity, choose a reduced-sodium soy sauce variant, and limit portion size to half a bowl to keep sodium intake below 700 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: The per-100g carbohydrate is low (2.36 g) and the composite meal GI is estimated at 54 (low); however, the full bowl with steamed white rice delivers approximately 60–70 g total carbohydrate, which will significantly raise postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, request a reduced rice portion (100 g cooked), and limit yakiniku sauce to minimise added sugar from mirin; the high protein content from beef and eggs supports glycaemic stability when carbohydrate is controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beef (red meat) contributes saturated fat and dietary cholesterol; two half-boiled eggs add approximately 370 mg dietary cholesterol per serving; yakiniku sauce adds sodium, all of which are relevant cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request reduced sauce, and pair with fibre-rich vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and cholesterol load from beef and eggs.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies pro-inflammatory TNF-alpha and IL-6 pathways relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise red meat consumption; substituting beef with grilled salmon or chicken and requesting additional anti-inflammatory vegetables (spinach, broccoli) would substantially reduce the inflammatory load of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double Half Boiled Egg Beef Yakiniku Bowl (ダブル半熟卵牛肉焼肉丼, Daburu Hanjuku Tamago Gyuniku Yakiniku Don; Chinese: 双溏心蛋牛肉烧肉饭) is a Japanese-style donburi rice bowl dish served in Singapore. It consists of steamed white rice topped with thinly sliced grilled beef marinated and glazed in yakiniku sauce — a sweet-savoury Japanese barbecue sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, garlic, and sesame — and crowned with two half-boiled (onsen-style) eggs whose soft, custard-like yolks enrich the dish with a creamy texture. The beef provides a caramelised, umami-forward flavour from the Maillard reaction during grilling, while the yakiniku sauce adds sweetness and depth. The half-boiled eggs contribute a silky, runny yolk that blends with the sauce to create a cohesive, rich coating over the rice. Per 100 g of the dish (as reported by HPB Singapore Foodo), the nutritional profile shows 55 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 2.36 g carbohydrate, and 0.0 g fat — a profile that likely reflects the protein-and-sauce component rather than the full bowl with rice. The full 400 g bowl is estimated to deliver approximately 220 kcal, 42 g protein, and 60–70 g carbohydrate. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium (~1,360 mg per bowl) due to the soy sauce base of yakiniku sauce, making it a concern for hypertension management. The white rice component raises the glycaemic load significantly, requiring portion control for Type 2 Diabetes patients. The red meat and egg cholesterol content are relevant for cardiovascular disease risk. The dish is culturally positioned as a Japanese-influenced fast-casual meal popular at lunch and dinner in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (G00010200) with confidence 0.95; however, the reported fat value of 0.0 g/100g is anomalous for a beef-and-egg dish and is suspected to reflect a data entry issue or a partial-component measurement rather than the full dish. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, sodium, sugar, fibre) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogues. GI is an analogous estimate. Overall confidence is medium due to the fat anomaly and estimated secondary nutrients.
notes: ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): fat_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB Foodo source (G00010200). This value is anomalous for a dish containing grilled beef and two half-boiled eggs, which would typically contribute 3–8 g fat/100g in a full bowl. The 0.0 g fat value is retained as reported from the approved HPB source but is flagged as likely reflecting the protein/sauce component only, not the full bowl with rice. Users should be aware the full dish fat content is likely higher. The fat_g value is not 'naturally zero' — it is a source-reported value that may be incomplete. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown for this specific Japanese beef yakiniku bowl preparation is not available in HPB Foodo G00010200 or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026-04-16. MULTILINGUAL NOTE: This dish is a Japanese-cuisine item served in Singapore. Mandatory Japanese local name is provided (ダブル半熟卵牛肉焼肉丼). Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are additionally provided to support Singapore multilingual retrieval given the Singapore serving context. GI CATEGORY NOTE: GI 54 is classified as low (≤55 threshold per University of Sydney GI Database convention); gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low'. ONTOLOGY NOTE: No exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for beef yakiniku donburi; approximate broader-class codes used and documented. SERVING SIZE NOTE: HPB Foodo does not specify a serving weight for G00010200; 400 g is estimated based on typical Japanese donburi serving sizes from USDA FoodData Central reference portions. SOURCE ANOMALY: The input record lists fat as 0.0 g/100g with HPB confidence 0.95; this is preserved as reported but flagged for review.

Added Malay local name (Mangkuk Nasi Daging Lembu Yakiniku dengan Dua Telur Separuh Masak) to local_names and aliases_by_language.malay to satisfy G6 and Q8.
Added Tamil local name (இரட்டை அரை வேகவைத்த முட்டை மாட்டிறைச்சி யாக்கினிகு கிண்ணம்) to local_names and aliases_by_language.tamil to satisfy G6 and Q8.
Added Hindi local name (डबल हाफ बॉयल्ड एग बीफ याकिनिकू बाउल) to local_names and aliases_by_language.hindi to satisfy G6 and Q8.
Added all new local-language strings verbatim to flat aliases array to satisfy Q8 consistency rule.
Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 54 falls at or below the 55 threshold for low GI per University of Sydney convention, addressing Q4 failure.
Updated gi_source notes to explicitly state GI 54 is in the low category.
Updated type2_diabetes health_context guidance to reference 'low' GI consistently.
Updated notes section to document gi_category correction and multilingual additions.
